metadata.title: Accelerated Decline in Hepatic Glucose Production During Fasting in Normal Women Compared With Men
metadata.study_design: Comparative metabolic study with sequential 3-3H-glucose infusions after 14 and 64-hour fasts
metadata.demographics: Five normal men and five normal women in the midfollicular phase of menstrual cycle, matched for age and body mass index, with no history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, kidney disease, or medication use

evidence.quote: five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel 3-3H-glucose infusions after both a 14 and a 64hour fast
women exhibit a significantly greater fall in Ra after 64 hours of fasting, which may account for the reportedly greater hypoglycemia during fasting in women

Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle. None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications. Subjects were matched for age and body mass index (BMI)
five normal men and five normal women underwent sequentiel S3H-glucose infusions
A primed (25 µCi) continuous (0.25 µCi) infusion of 3-3H-glucose (New England Nuclear, Boston) was administered via a forearm vein from 7 AM until 10 AM
blood samples were obtained at 20-minute intervals during the last hour of the study (9 AM to 10 AM)

methods_participants.label: Plasma glucose measurement method
methods_participants.method: Gammacoat assay, Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose was measured immediately after centrifugation by the glucose oxidase method (Beckman, Fullerton, CA)
Plasma insulin, C-peptide, growth hormone, and glucagon were determined using double-antibody radioimmunoassays

methods_participants.label: Inclusion criterion - menstrual cycle phase
methods_participants.value: None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications

evidence.quote: None had a history of diabetes mellitus, hypertension, or kidney disease or were on any medications
Subjects were matched for age and body mass index (BMI)
Body composition was assessed by skinfold measurements and impedance plethysmography (BIA-101 Body Composition Analyzer, RJL Systems, Detroit)
During the two-hour equilibration period required for glucose kinetic studies (subjects reclining)
Five of the subjects were male and five were female in the midfollicular phase of their menstrual cycle

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men and women

evidence.quote: Plasma glucose values fell by 24% from the 16th to the 64th hour of fasting in both men (5.23 + 0.03 v 3.96 + 0.14 mmol/L, P < .OOl) and women (4.84 + 0.14 v 3.65 f 0.25 mmol/L, P < .OOl).

narrative_results.statement: Plasma glycerol levels were significantly greater in women compared with men after the 64-hour fast

evidence.quote: Glucose clearance rates did not change significantly in either sex after the 64-hour fast

narrative_results.statement: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon

evidence.quote: Changes in plasma glucose were associated with decreases in plasma insulin and C-peptide as well as increases in plasma glucagon (Table 2).

narrative_results.statement: 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted

evidence.quote: 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted

discrepancies.pass_2_value: Pass_1 describes continuous infusion dose (0.25 µCi); pass_2 describes infusion duration (3 hours); these are different parameters misaligned
Pass_1 describes infusion duration (3 hours); pass_2 describes primed dose (25 µCi); fundamentally different parameters

Pass 1 states multiple exclusion criteria (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease, medications). Pass 2 only mentions diabetes mellitus. Pass 2 appears to be incomplete extraction of the same criterion.

Pass 1 lists only skinfold measurements. Pass 2 lists both skinfold measurements AND impedance plethysmography with device details. These appear to be different extractions of overlapping but distinct information.
Pass 1 reports complete participant count (5 men and 5 women total). Pass 2 reports only male participants (5 men). These are incompatible representations of the study population.

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: Both the absolute (0.55 + 0.06 v 0.32 * 0.06 mg.kg-'.min-') and the percent fall in Ra (24.6% + 1.4% v 15.8% t 3.0%, Fig 1, inset) were significantly greater in women compared with men (P -e .05).

structural_discrepancies.pass_2_value: Plasma glycerol levels rose significantly in both men and women during the 64-hour fast

structural_discrepancies.pass_1_value: In conclusion, 64-hour fasted women exhibit a greater fall in HGO than do men similarly fasted.

semantic_groups.rationale: Both describe 14-hour fasting duration for first study; only labeling differs
Both describe 64-hour fasting duration for second study; only labeling differs
Both describe glucose tracer infusion with 3-3H-glucose; pass_1 specifies primed dose (25 µCi) while pass_2 describes method more generally
Pass_1 describes continuous infusion dose (0.25 µCi); pass_2 describes infusion duration (3 hours); these are different parameters misaligned
Pass_1 describes infusion duration (3 hours); pass_2 describes primed dose (25 µCi); fundamentally different parameters
Both describe 20-minute blood sampling intervals; minor wording variation in presentation
Both describe glucose oxidase method for plasma glucose measurement; pass_1 includes manufacturer details (Beckman, Fullerton, CA) while pass_2 abbreviates to Beckman analyzer
Identical description of double-antibody radioimmunoassay for insulin measurement
Same cortisol measurement method (direct radioimmunoassay, Gammacoat assay). Pass 1 includes manufacturer details (Clinical Assays, Cambridge, MA) while Pass 2 omits them.
Pass 1 contains inclusion criterion about age (normal volunteers), but Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.

Pass 1 states multiple exclusion criteria (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease, medications). Pass 2 only mentions diabetes mellitus. Pass 2 appears to be incomplete extraction of the same criterion.
Both describe the same inclusion criterion for female menstrual cycle phase (midfollicular phase). Minor labeling differences only.
Pass 1 contains matching criterion (age and BMI), but Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.

Pass 1 lists only skinfold measurements. Pass 2 lists both skinfold measurements AND impedance plethysmography with device details. These appear to be different extractions of overlapping but distinct information.
Pass 1 contains body composition assessment method 2 (impedance plethysmography with device details), but Pass 2 is empty. Information only in one extraction.
Pass 1 reports complete participant count (5 men and 5 women total). Pass 2 reports only male participants (5 men). These are incompatible representations of the study population.
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains study population data (5 normal women)
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains glucose infusion dosing information
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains inclusion criteria regarding hypertension
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains inclusion criteria regarding kidney disease
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains inclusion criteria regarding medications
Pass 1 is empty; only Pass 2 contains equilibration period information
Identical statements about glucose appearance rates falling during 64-hour fast in both sexes
Pass 1 specifies 'percent fall' while Pass 2 says 'fall'; both convey same finding of greater decrease in women
Identical statements about glucose clearance rates not changing significantly after fast
Identical statements about associations between plasma glucose changes and hormone levels
Identical statements about plasma glycerol levels being greater in women after fast
Pass 1 says 'beta-hydroxybutyrate' while Pass 2 specifies 'plasma β-hydroxybutyrate levels'; same fact with minor specification difference
Identical statements about women showing greater fall in HGO than men during 64-hour fast
Identical statements about 24% plasma glucose decline from 16th to 64th hour of fasting
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific finding about plasma glycerol levels during fasting. No conflicting information, but one side is missing.
Pass 1 is empty; Pass 2 contains a specific finding about plasma FFA levels and intersex differences. No conflicting information, but one side is missing.
